title: Lavardo Whylly Vs. Commissioner of Police
text:
1
1 COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
2 IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
3 MCCrApp & CAIS No. 127 of 2024
4
5 Lavardo Whylly
6 Intended Appellant
7 vs
8
9 The Commissioner of Police
10 Intended Respondent
11
12 (Extension of Time
Substantive Appeal)
13
14
15
Before: The Hon Mr Justice Evans, President
16 The Hon Mr Justice Smith, JA
The Hon Madam Justice Fraser, JA
17
18 Intended Appellant appeared Pro Se
Ms Shaneka Carey, Counsel for Intended Respondent
19
20
21
22 9 March 2026
23
24
25

1 The oral judgment of the court was delivered by
2 Smith, JA:
3 The intended appellant has filed an application for
4 an extension of time within which to appeal. I will refer to
5 him from now on as Lavardo Whylly.
6 The basic background facts are that Lavardo Whylly
7 was charged with the offence of wantonly discharging
8 a firearm, contrary to section 37 of the Firearms Act, Chapter
9 213.
10 The brief facts are that on 9th October, 2022,
11 Corporal Andrew Demeritte was at a homecoming event at
12 Wemyss Bight Community Park when he heard a loud bang of what
13 sounded like a gunshot being discharged. He looked over the
14 fence and saw the appellant, Mr. Whylly, with an object in his
15 hand. Mr. Whylly then discharged two more gunshots into the
16 air from a brown-coloured firearm. Mr. Whylly was dashed to
17 the ground by a female and told to leave the park. The
18 incident was then reported to the police, and Mr. Whylly was
19 arrested.
20 In addition to that, there was the evidence of
21 Inspector Farquharson, who indicated that Mr. Whylly admitted
22 to him orally that he did have the gun, and I quote that he
23 says:
24 "That gun was passed to me. I tried to get at
him, but my girl stopped me. I ran hot and bust
25 a couple of shots in the air."

3
1 Inspector Farquharson stated that Mr. Whylly
2 described the firearm as being a small brownish pistol, and he
3 directed himself and other officers to the Wemyss Bight
4 Community Park, where he pointed out an area where he said he
5 threw the gun. However, the firearm was not discovered.
6 Inspector Maurice Arthur also testified that he was
7 present when Mr. Whylly made this admission to
8 Inspector Farquharson.
9 Mr. Whylly gave evidence in this matter. When
10 questioned whether he had an exchange of words with anyone in
11 the park, he said:
12 "I was under the influence of alcohol and drugs,
so I can't recall."
13
"What kind of drugs were you under the influence
14 of on the night of the incident?
15 "The regular. I does smoke herb."
16 At the time, he admitted to being high.
17 Further, the case was put to him that he had
18 a dispute with another male. As a result, he discharged the
19 firearm into the air and also he threw the firearm in the
20 bush. His case was not a denial of the case that was put to
21 him, but rather, that no shell casings were found.
22 The magistrate, in his sentencing remarks, said that
23 he preferred the evidence of the prosecution over the defence.
24 The magistrate specifically stated:
25 "Having heard witnesses for the prosecution, the
court finds the witnesses to be truthful and

4
1 "credible. None of the witnesses was discredited
in the process of giving their evidence. The
2 court accepts prosecution s case that
Officer Demeritte to whom the defendant was well
3 known, saw the defendant with a firearm in his
hand, which he then discharged into the air a
4 couple of times at a public gathering on park,
before leaving the scene on foot.
5
"The court also accepts the evidence of
6 Inspector Farquharson under oath that
[Mr. Whylly] admitted to him that on the night of
7 the incident, he possessed a firearm, which he
discharged in the air, because a Rasta male was
8 interfering with him."
9 On this information, the magistrate found:
10 "The court prefers the evidence of prosecution
over that of the defendant in finding that
11 prosecution proved its case to the requisite
standard, beyond reasonable doubt, that on
12 October 9, 2022, the defendant wantonly
discharged a firearm to the annoyance and danger
13 of persons gathered on the park, including
Corporal Demeritte."
14
15 We see no error or fault, or any reason to disagree
16 with the findings of the learned magistrate with respect to
17 conviction.
18 With respect to sentence, the magistrate considered
19 the age of Mr. Whylly, that he had a nine-year-old and
20 16-year-old dependant; that he was employed at the time of the
21 offence as semi-skilled carpenter, but he also noted that he
22 had a prior conviction for a similar offence and also the
23 aggravating factors that the offence took place in a public
24 place where there was a gathering of persons attending
25 a homecoming event and that the firearm was not recovered.

5
1 In short, the magistrate did consider the mitigating
2 and aggravating factors. We also know that the maximum
3 sentence for this offence is seven years, and the magistrate
4 imposed a sentence of three years, which we find was
5 reasonable in all the circumstances, and we see no error of
6 principle committed by the magistrate in sentencing.
7 In the circumstances, we see no reason to grant an
8 extension of time to appeal, as we are of the opinion that the
9 appeal does not have any reasonable prospects of success.
10 In the circumstances, we dismiss the application for
11 an extension of time, and we affirm the conviction and
12 sentence of this appellant, Mr. Lavardo Whylly.
13 That is the decision of the court.
14
15
16 Dated this 9th day of March, 2026
17
18 _________________________
19 SMITH, JA
20
21 ss
22
23
24
25
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas 

 
 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 Return to Main Page
summary: In the circumstances, we dismiss the application for an extension of time, and we affirm the conviction and sentence of this appellant, Mr. Lavardo Whylly. That is the decision of the court.
